article:
In brief: it's going to be a warm and humid week ahead for Abuja, with temperatures staying in the low to mid-30s and a good chance of rain showers starting from Friday. If you're planning on visiting the National Assembly Complex or strolling through the Millennium Park, make sure to pack accordingly and stay hydrated. Today, though, is looking relatively calm, with plenty of clouds but not much wind to speak of.

As we take a look at the current conditions, it's clear that the heat index is playing a big role in how the temperature feels. At 23°C, it's not terribly hot, but with the humidity sitting at 92%, it feels more like 28°C. This is something we're quite used to in Abuja, especially during this time of year. The winds are very light, barely registering at 2 km/h, which means that any breeze you might feel is likely coming from a passing cloud or the ventilation system in your car or building.

If you're planning on spending any time outdoors today, you shouldn't have to worry too much about the sun beating down on you. The UV index is currently at 0, which is as low as it gets. This is partially due to the cloud cover, but also because we're still in the midst of the dry season, when the sun's rays aren't quite as intense. That being said, it's always a good idea to wear sunscreen and protective clothing if you're going to be outside for an extended period, even on cloudy days.

### 2026-04-03
Looking ahead to tomorrow, we're expecting a high of 32°C and a low of 23°C, with just a 20% chance of rain. This should make for a pleasant day, especially if you're planning on visiting some of Abuja's many markets, such as the Wuse Market or the Garki Market. The sun should be shining, and while it will undoubtedly be warm, it shouldn't be unbearably so. [MAP: Satellite image of Abuja, showing cloud cover and temperature gradients]

As we head into the day, keep an eye on the skies, as there is a slight chance of isolated showers. These will likely be brief and confined to specific areas, but it's always a good idea to have an umbrella or rain jacket handy, just in case. The winds will remain light, blowing at around 5 km/h, which should help to make the heat feel a bit more bearable.

### 2026-04-04
On Friday, our attention turns to a bit of a rain chance. We're looking at a 35% chance of precipitation, with a high of 29°C and a low of 25°C. This could make for a bit of a cooler day, especially if the rain materializes. If you're planning on driving, be sure to exercise caution, as the roads can get quite slippery when wet. [MAP: Radar image of Abuja, showing precipitation patterns and movement]

The rain chance is due to a low-pressure system that's moving into the area, bringing with it a mass of moist air from the Gulf of Guinea. This system will stall over us for the next few days, leading to an increased chance of showers and thunderstorms. While it's not looking like a complete washout, it's definitely something to be aware of, especially if you have outdoor plans.

### 2026-04-05
On Saturday, the pattern continues, with a 33% chance of rain and a high of 32°C. The low will dip down to 25°C, making for a relatively comfortable evening, especially if you're planning on enjoying the nightlife in areas like Jabi or Wuse II. The rain chance will again be driven by the low-pressure system, which will continue to dominate our weather pattern for the next few days.

As we head into the weekend, it's a good idea to stay flexible with your plans, in case the rain does decide to make an appearance. If you're attending any outdoor events or meetings, be sure to have a backup plan in place, or at the very least, a tent or indoor space to fall back on. [MAP: Forecast model image, showing the position and movement of the low-pressure system]

### 2026-04-06
On Sunday, we're looking at a high of 34°C and a low of 27°C, with a 30% chance of rain. This should make for a warm day, but the heat index will likely be more manageable than it has been in recent days, thanks to a slight decrease in humidity. If you're planning on visiting the Abuja National Mosque or the Nigerian National Museum, be sure to stay hydrated and take breaks in the shade or air conditioning.

The rain chance will continue to be driven by the low-pressure system, which will begin to move slowly out of the area by the end of the day. As it does, we can expect the skies to begin clearing, leading to a bit more sunshine and warmer temperatures.

### 2026-04-07
On Monday, we're looking at a high of 34°C and a low of 26°C, with a 45% chance of rain. This is the highest chance of precipitation we've seen all week, and it's likely that we'll see some more widespread showers and thunderstorms. If you're commuting to work or school, be sure to leave a bit earlier than usual, in case the roads are slowed down by rain.

The increased rain chance is due to the low-pressure system, which will still be lingering in the area, combining with some moisture from the south to produce more showers and storms. While it's not looking like a complete washout, it's definitely something to be aware of, especially if you have outdoor plans or are planning on traveling.

### 2026-04-08
On Tuesday, the rain chance will begin to decrease, dropping down to 44%. The high will remain at 34°C, while the low will dip down to 25°C. This should make for a bit of a more pleasant day, especially if you're planning on spending time outdoors. If you're visiting the Aso Rock or the Three Arms Zone, be sure to take in the views and enjoy the fresh air.

The decrease in rain chance is due to the low-pressure system finally beginning to move out of the area, taking the bulk of the moisture with it. As it does, we can expect the skies to begin clearing, leading to more sunshine and warmer temperatures.

### 2026-04-09
On Wednesday, we're looking at a high of 32°C and a low of 25°C, with a 60% chance of rain. This is the highest chance of precipitation we've seen all week, and it's likely that we'll see some more widespread showers and storms. If you're planning on attending any outdoor events or meetings, be sure to have a backup plan in place, or at the very least, a tent or indoor space to fall back on.

The increased rain chance is due to another low-pressure system that's moving into the area, bringing with it a mass of moist air from the Gulf of Guinea. This system will stall over us for the next few days, leading to an increased chance of showers and thunderstorms. While it's not looking like a complete washout, it's definitely something to be aware of, especially if you have outdoor plans. [MAP: Forecast model image, showing the position and movement of the new low-pressure system]
